text: laparoscopic excision of second and third part duodenal junction large tumor a middle-aged female with a history of pain in a right side no weight loss no appetite loss no jaundice or fever ultrasound suggested a large duodenal mass the findings were confirmed on ct scan with exophytic mass arising from the d-to-d-three junction and there was an ulcer which led to oral contrast within the mass these are the ct scan images we can see the oral contrast going within the mass Upper GI scopy was done just to confirm and if possible to take the biopsy but it was not possible. EUS showed the nexophytic highly vascular lesion.
This was the ulcer with the food content coming out of it and there was no visible mass. Hence the patient was taken up for the surgery and excision of the gist.
Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon lifts the transverse colon superiorly, revealing a highly vascular mass suspected to be a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST).

The surgeon begins dissection at the superior aspect of the mass, revealing the second and third parts of the duodenum. The transverse colon is carefully reflected superiorly, exposing the anterior surface of the duodenum.

The surgeon progresses the dissection towards the inferior aspect of the mass at the ileocolic junction, attempting to visualize the ileocolic vessel. The GIST tumor is observed as a pushing tumor, allowing the surgeon to identify and preserve the surrounding vessels by maintaining the proper dissection plane.
The surgeon uses a bipolar energy sealing device to tackle the highly vascular mass located at the duodenal junction.

The surgeon carefully reflects the ileocolic vessels from the capsulated mass, revealing the pulsation of the vessels. Dissection is performed at the inferior border of the mass, creating a visible window in the mesentery of the colon.
The surgeon enters the retroperitoneum, revealing a highly vascular tumor with multiple visible vessels surrounding it.

lying all over the mass so care should be taken to avoid any injury because that can lead to torrential bleeding and it will spoil the entire field of surgery so one must try and remain as close as possible to the mass in order to avoid the injury to the surrounding important structures at the same time one must take care of avoiding hemorrhage happening from
The surgeon exposes the medial end of the colon, revealing the mass and associated vessels, which are part of the ileocolic region.
Patience is the key in this kind of surgery. And if we have a vessel sealing device and a good vascular forceps, one can always do this variety of surgery, even with laparoscopy.

Blood pressure again also is the key. Our anesthetist was very friendly enough to keep the systolic blood pressure around ninety and mean more than seventy so that the urine output is maintained. At the same time, you can appreciate the surgical field that remains neat and clean despite such a highly vascular tumor.

The surgeon progresses with the dissection on the anterior or superior aspect of the tumor, revealing the transverse colon. Care is taken to preserve the vascular arcade of the colon during this procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the attachment between the mass and the mesocolon, pushing the colon away to reveal the lifted section of the colon.

The surgeon identifies and divides the right colic vessel and a supplying vessel to the gastrointestinal stromal tumor using a ligature device. The dissection continues on the superior aspect of the tumor.
The surgeon lifts the colon, separating it from the mesocolon, and reflects the tumor medially. The second and third parts of the duodenum are visible, with the tumor located at the D2-D3 junction.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to divide the duodenum, thinning out the base of the gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). The tumor is visibly separated from the duodenum, with minor bleeding observed during the process.

The surgical field is cleaned, and the surgeon closes a small enterotomy using 3-0 Vicryl sutures. The healthy, normal mucosa is visible as the surgeon places a central stitch first, followed by stitches on either side of the duodenum. The mesentery of the bowel remains undisturbed, allowing for primary closure of the duodenum without additional anastomosis or diversion procedures.
The surgeon places multiple interrupted sutures around the entrotomy site, completing the closure with the final few stitches.
The completed suturing is visible in the clean surgical field. A drain is placed near the surgical area, and the specimen, identified as a high-grade GIST, was retrieved.
